title: The dimensions of data quality
description: Quality spans accuracy, completeness, consistency, timeliness, validity, and uniqueness; a dataset can pass one and fail another.
body: Use when deciding what to measure. Quality is multi-dimensional: accuracy (matches reality), completeness (no missing rows/fields), consistency (agrees across tables), timeliness/freshness (recent enough), validity (conforms to schema/rules), and uniqueness (no unintended dupes). A table can be perfectly fresh and valid yet incomplete, or unique yet inaccurate. Do not equate 'schema passes' with 'data is good'. Actions: pick checks across the dimensions that matter for the use case. Validation signals include explicit checks mapped to each relevant dimension. Risk boundary: measuring only one dimension leaves blind spots that surface as wrong downstream results; cover the dimensions the decision depends on.
